coredata.dc:title: Molecularly designed and synthesized of bright blue nitrogen-doped lignin-derived carbon dots applied in printable anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiaohui
Yang, Rendang
Wang, Yang
Cheng, Chen
Fu, Danning
Sheng, Jie
coredata.dc:description: With the increased demand for green and sustainable development, the research of advanced biomass-based carbon dots (CDs) has drawn growing attention. Herein, a one-step green solvent integration strategy-assisted solvothermal method to preparing CDs from hydrolyzed lignin and ethylenediamine (EDA) in formamide (FA) was developed. The Schiff reaction between FA and EDA contributes to the formation of -C-N groups, further inducing the high photoluminescence quantum yield (up to 42.69 %)，obviously higher than NCDs prepared in H2O, EtOH and DMF systems (corresponding to H-NCDs, E-NCDs and D-NCDs, respectively). The analysis of structure, composition, photoluminescence (PL) behaviors and DFT calculations showed that F-NCDs have main blue fluorescent emission peak from 410 to 455 nm under 330–390 nm excitation due to the small sp2 structure in carbon core, and the large sp2 conjugated clusters and CO group related surface states leaded to the long wavelength emission. The F-NCDs with excellent optical properties was further used for preparing fluorescent film and invisible anti-counterfeiting ink, which exhibited outstanding fluorescence even at different temperatures and aging times. We provided a facile way for green facile preparation of lignin-based CDs and their sustainable anti-counterfeiting application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin-derived carbon dots
Fluorescent tuning mechanism
Printable anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the anti-counterfeiting waterborne ink
Effect of chemical structure on PL emission of NCDs
Applications of fluorescent film and anti-counterfeiting ink

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2022A1515111147 ) and State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering ( 202309 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, high-resolution TEM images (Top inset) and size distribution histograms of (a) F-NCDs, (b) H-NCDs, (c) E-NCDs, and (d) D-NCDs.

Normalized PL emission spectra of (a) F-NCDs, (b) H-NCDs, (c) E-NCDs and (d) D-NCDs aqueous solutions excited at their optimal excitation wavelengths, with concentrations of 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg·mL−1.

(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) high-resolution XPS C1s spectra, (c) high-resolution XPS N1s spectra and (d) high-resolution XPS O1s spectra of F-NCDs. Time resolved fluorescence decay curves of (e) F-NCDs, (f) H-NCDs, (g) E-NCDs and (h) D-NCDs.
Schematic illustration of solvent integration of lignin-based NCDs in FA system.

Adsorption spectra of (a) F-NCDs, (b) H-NCDs, (c) E-NCDs, and (d) D-NCDs with different concentrations. Tauc plots of (e) F-NCDs, (f) H-NCDs, (g) E-NCDs, and (h) D-NCDs solutions with different concentrations. (i) Energy-level and FL emission position of NCDs. Illustration of energy transfer of NCDs (j-m).
Calculated LUMO-HOMO orbitals of structural models of CDs with different numbers of conjugated rings, -OH, CO, -NH2 and -C-N groups at the B3LYP/6-31 g (d, p) level.
The effect of (a) pH values and (b) ionic strength on the PL intensity of F-NCDs.

(a) Photos of films: (i) pattern, (ii) F-NCDs-PVA transparent film on the pattern under day light and (iii) UV irradiation (365 nm); photos of F-NCDs based for anti-counterfeiting: (b) polypropylene paper, (c) pulp board and (d) calcium silicate board under (i) day light and (ii-iv) UV irradiation (365 nm).

Photos of (a-b) films and (c-d) printed characters under UV (365 nm) light for (a, c) different temperature (RT, −40°C, 0°C, 40°C, −80°C) and (b, d) aging times (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 h). RT means “Room temperature”.
Relative contents of chemical bonds in NCDs analyzed by XPS.

head.ce:title: Molecularly designed and synthesized of bright blue nitrogen-doped lignin-derived carbon dots applied in printable anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Foshan University, Foshan 528000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Foshan University, Foshan 528000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increased demand for green and sustainable development, the research of advanced biomass-based carbon dots (CDs) has drawn growing attention. Herein, a one-step green solvent integration strategy-assisted solvothermal method to preparing CDs from hydrolyzed lignin and ethylenediamine (EDA) in formamide (FA) was developed. The Schiff reaction between FA and EDA contributes to the formation of -C-N groups, further inducing the high photoluminescence quantum yield (up to 42.69 %)，obviously higher than NCDs prepared in H2O, EtOH and DMF systems (corresponding to H-NCDs, E-NCDs and D-NCDs, respectively). The analysis of structure, composition, photoluminescence (PL) behaviors and DFT calculations showed that F-NCDs have main blue fluorescent emission peak from 410 to 455 nm under 330–390 nm excitation due to the small sp2 structure in carbon core, and the large sp2 conjugated clusters and CO group related surface states leaded to the long wavelength emission. The F-NCDs with excellent optical properties was further used for preparing fluorescent film and invisible anti-counterfeiting ink, which exhibited outstanding fluorescence even at different temperatures and aging times. We provided a facile way for green facile preparation of lignin-based CDs and their sustainable anti-counterfeiting application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used are available from the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with size below 10 nm [1], which can be treated as a nanocrystalline core with oxygenic moieties on the surface [2]. CDs have exhibited important momentum in numerous fields, owing to their benefits as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, feasible surface modification, and outstanding photostability [3]. CDs have become a more promising new-generation luminescent materials compared to fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [4]. However, it is still an urgent concern that CDs based on inorganic precursors induce environment pollutions and impediments to sustainability [5]. Therefore, the conversion of green and inexpensive resources (biomass and its components) to CDs is particularly instrumental for sustainable development [6].

Profiting from its renewability, aromatic structure, and abundant endogenous heteroatom doping, lignin is considered as a promising biomass material for CDs [7,8] with a growing hotspot [9]. In general, lignin is underutilized in the traditional pulping and papermaking industries, and being carbonized for heat as a low value by-product [10]. Lignin can be classified into hydrolyzed lignin, alkali lignin, kraft lignin, organosolv lignin and lignosulfonates with differing chemical structures and compositions. Nowadays, many efforts have been made to overcome the natural resistance of lignin for preparation lignin-based CDs. The synthesis process also includes acidolysis and aromatic repolymerization [11–15]. Acid additives are used for chemical cutting, such as H2SO4, HNO3, 3-aminophenylboronic acid, and o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid. However, the acidolysis with corrosive and unrecyclable acid was time-consuming and tedious, inducing the condensation reactions of lignin [16], and further posing a threat to our ecological environment and human health. Therefore, the proper utilization of lignin and the effective synthesis strategy need be further investigated for advanced CDs.

To date, several strategies have been developed for photoluminescence (PL) enhancement, such as heteroatom doping, synthesis regulation (raw materials, reaction temperature, or time), solvent-controlled strategy and concentration tunability. Heteroatom doping emerges as an effective strategy to improve optical properties of CDs by engineering multiple defect states [4]. Such as, Yang et al. prepared a lignin-derived carbon dots with excellent green fluorescence and sensitive pH responsiveness, due to the introduction of magnesium and nitrogen related defect states [17]. The reaction solvents with differing physicochemical traits (boiling points, solubility, hydrophilicities, polarity, protic or aprotic) controlled the dehydration and carbonization process, core and surface structures of CDs [18–20]. Liu et al. proposed that tunable luminescence of lignin-based carbon dots can be realized in formamide and ethanol reaction solvents due to the different graphitization and surface functionalization of CDs [18]. However, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of lignin-based CDs was rather low, and the functionalization and utilization of CDs need be further explored. In addition, the concentration-dependent PL emission plays a vital role in investigating the fluorescence mechanism of CDs [21–26]. For example, Meng et al., tuned the emission wavelengths of CDs from 630 nm to 400 nm related to concentration-dependent PL emission [21]; Zhang et al., reported that CDs exhibiting tunable fluorescence were prepared by tuning concentration due to the modified band gaps and -C-O-C/C-O-H groups of CDs [22]. With regarding to lignin-based CDs, the concentration-dependent fluorescence behaviors need be explored in depth, which will favor the exploration of their fluorescence mechanism and regulation for further application.

With that in mind, the synergy of reaction solvent and nitrogen dopant is expected to be devoted to luminescence enhancement of lignin-based CDs. In view of the high reactivity of polar protonated formamide (FA), the Schiff reaction may occur between -C=O of FA and -NH2 of nitrogen dopant, leading to the formation of new -C-N groups and N-related emission states for CDs. In this study, a green and feasible one-step solvothermal route was developed to prepare N-doped lignin-derived carbon dots (NCDs) in different non corrosive acid solvent systems (H2O, FA, ethanol, and DMF), using hydrolyzed lignin and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. It is worth noting that the Schiff reaction between -NH2 of EDA and CO of FA contributes to effectively increasing the -C-N groups in F-NCDs (N-doped lignin-based CDs in formamide). As a consequence, F-NCDs were endowed with the uniform morphology, excellent blue PL emission, and high PLQY (42.69 %). Through the analysis of structures, chemical compositions and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the concentration-dependent PL emission behaviors of four NCDs were explored for the future accurate design of target CDs products. Based on the great optical properties and photostability of F-NCDs, the fluorescent film and invisible two-dimensional (2D) markings with anti-counterfeiting function were developed by combining screen printing technology using the prepared ink. This work hopes to open a facile way for the green feasible preparation of lignin-based CDs with excellent PL performances and promoting their anti-counterfeiting application with high accuracy.

Poplar wood ships were provided by the State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (Guangzhou, China). The deionized water (H2O) was made in our laboratory. Formamide (FA) was obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd., N, N-Dimethyl formamide (DMF) were obtained from Richjoint Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl alcohol (EtOH, 99.7%) was purchased from Titian Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). H2SO4, ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%), KCl and NaOH were obtained from Reagent Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Polyvinyl alcohol 124 (PVA 124) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Organic silicon defoamer was purchased from Macklin Biochemical CO., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate and urea were purchased from FUCHEN Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The surface morphology of NCDs was investigated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM 2100F, JEOL, Japan). The morphology and element fractions of NCDs-PVA membrane were obtained from Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Quanta FEG 250, Hillsboro, OR, USA) equipped with an X-ray spectroscopy instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a TENSOR27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using a standard potassium bromide (KBr) pellet technique. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by Cu Kα X-ray radiation on an X-ray diffractometer (D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). The molecular weight distribution and average molecular of the acetylated lignin were determined by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Agilent 1260 LC, Germany) equipped with a RID 1A differential refractometer and a PL gel 6 μm 103 Å column (300 mm × 7.8 mm) with THF as 100 % eluent (1 mL/min) at 30°C. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by Cu Kα X-ray radiation on an electron spectrophotometer (Axis Supra+, KRATOS, UK). The optical absorption spectra were obtained on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-1900, SHIMADZU, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) properties were determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Scientific Fluoromax-4, HORIBA, USA). The pH and ionic strength stability of CDs were determined by the fluorescence spectrophotometer at different pH (2−12) and KCl solutions (0–1 M). The PLQYs and PL decays were detected on an Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK). In density functional theory (DFT) calculation, all the geometry optimization were performed at the level B3LYP and 6-31G (d) basis set in the vacuum phase. The frequency calculations on the optimized structures were carried out at the same conditions. The Gaussian 09 program were used to perform the above calculations. The Gauss view 5 software was used to generate the molecular orbitals.

Lignin was extracted from the hydrolysate of poplar wood chips by acid precipitation. The details of hydrolysis were as follows: 500 g of dry wood chips, the set heating temperature was 170°C, liquor/solid of 6:1 (w/w), the heat-up and holding time were both 60 min. After hydrolysis, the hydrolysate was separated from the mixture by filtration through a 200-mesh strainer. The hydrolysate was submitted to lignin precipitation by adding H2SO4 to pH = 2. The crude precipitates were then centrifuged and washed with water to neutral. After lyophilization, the final hydrolyzed lignin was prepared.

0.1 g lignin was dispersed in 2 mL pyridine/acetic anhydride (v/v, 1:1). The mixture was sealed and reacted at room temperature for 72 h. The obtained acetylated lignin was precipitated in HCl solution (pH = 1), stirring constantly, filtrated by organic filter film (0.22 μm), washed with deionized water, and vacuum-dried at 40°C.

The NCDs were prepared from hydrolyzed lignin and EDA by solvothermal carbonization in different solvent systems (H2O, FA, EtOH and DMF). As shown in Fig. S1, an appropriate mass ratio was 50 mg of lignin and 600 μL of EDA. The synthetic details were as follows. 50 mg lignin was dissolved in 20 mL FA and sonicated (800 W) 5 min for dispersion, then added 600 μL EDA and stirred continuously for 10 min. The mixture was transferred to the poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180°C for 10 h. Afterwards, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large particles. To obtain the pure NCDs, the supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm organic membrane filter and dialyzed with regenerated cellulose membrane (MWCO: 500 Da) in deionized water for 48 h to remove the solvent and impurities. Finally, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and lyophilized to obtain the NCDs powders, which were named as F-NCDs.
Three other NCDs were prepared by changing the reaction solvents (i.e., H2O, EtOH, and DMF) with the other parameters fixed and the products were named as H-NCDs, E-NCDs, and D-NCDs, respectively.

5 g PVA was firstly dissolved in 100 mL deionized water under stirring at 95°C for 2 h to obtain the transparent solution. Then, 5 mg F-NCDs powder was mixed with the PVA solution under stirring at room temperature for 30 min to obtain the F-NCDs-PVA solution. 2 mL F-NCDs-PVA solution was dropped into a plastic petri dish (Diameter: 3.5 cm) and dried at 40°C for 24 h. Then, transparent and fluorescent F-NCDs-PVA films were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the anti-counterfeiting waterborne ink
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 wt% silicone defoamer, 1 wt% sodium dodecyl sulfate and urea were then added to the 5 wt% PVA solution at 45°C under stirring. 1 wt% F-NCDs was added to the mixture, further gently stirred at 45°C for 1 h. The obtained waterborne ink was obtained as fluorescent F-NCDs-PVA ink.

Fig. 1a-d showed the TEM images of the four NCDs at concentration of 0.1 mg·mL−1. The results showed that the NCDs samples were both spherical, and the average sizes of F-NCDs, H-NCDs, E-NCDs were 3.24, 3.42 and 4.46 nm, respectively, showing a normal distribution. While the size distribution of D-NCDs could be fitted to two peaks with the average values of 3.13 and 7.11 nm. The small size value corresponded to the dispersed D-NCDs in aqueous solution，and the large size was assigned to the aggregates of the dispersed D-NCDs (Fig. S6). The poor dispersion of D-NCDs may attribute to the hydrogen bonding between surface groups and H2O molecule [27] to reduce interfacial energy, consistent with the previous study of Liu et al. [28]. The inset of Fig. 1a-d showed the lattice fringes of 0.220, 0.236, 0.223 and 0.341 nm, which fitted with the (102), (1120), (102) and (002) plane of graphitic carbon [29–31], respectively. These indicated that the solvent will significantly impact the carbon core structure of NCDs. From the perspective of particle size, the high aggregation degrees of NCDs are corresponded to the large particle size and the long emission wavelengths, which is consistent with quantum confinement effect (QCE), stating that the increase of particle size will induce the redshift in emission wavelength [32].

The molecular weights of different lignin in poplar were listed in Table S1. Compared with the typical isolated lignin, such as milled wood lignin (MWL), ionic liquid lignin (ILL), and alkali lignin (AL), the average weight molecular weight (Mw) and number molecular weight (Mn) of the hydrolyzed lignin were 1214 and 828, respectively, and the polydispersity index (PDI) was 1.47. These indicated that the hydrolyzed lignin was less resistant for preparing CDs than others. Fig. S7a showed the X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles of samples. The XRD patterns of NCDs showed a large broad diffraction peak (~22.5°) as compared to lignin (21.6°), indicating that the smaller particle size of NCDs than that of lignin [33]. The calculated d spacing of NCDs ranged from 3.91 to 4.12 Å, distinct from lignin (4.19 Å) and graphite (3.44 Å). The different d spacing between graphite and NCDs was attributed to the -OH, -C-H and -C-O-R introduced during the synthesis of NCDs. From the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S7b), for the initial lignin, the absorption peak at 3434 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of OH group, the peaks at 2934 and 2840 cm−1 were owing to the CH groups, the peaks at 1714 and 1608 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of non-conjugated CO in ketone and aromatic nucleus with CO, respectively. Peaks at 1514 and 1458 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of aromatic nucleus and deformation vibration peak of CH in alkyl. As for NCDs, the 3224–3590 cm−1 broad absorption bands were attributed to the overlapping association peaks of OH and NH groups, stating that there were numerous -OH and -NH2 groups in NCDs. The peak at about 1647 cm−1 could be assigned to the association peak of CC and CN groups, implying the presence of aromatic nitrogen structures [3]. The emerging peaks of NCDs at 1512 and 1457 cm−1 were corresponding to the bending vibration of CN and C-N-C, respectively. The enhanced peaks of CN and C-N-C donated the high doped N in the structure of NCDs. The absorption peak between 1211 and 1109 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of CO and C-O-C bonds that attributed to the nature structure of lignin [34]. While the peak of CO in NCDs spectra cannot be detected due to the hyperconjugation between surface functional groups and graphited‑carbon core [35].

To demonstrate the concentration-dependent PL emission of the four NCDs, NCDs aqueous solutions with the same concentration gradients were prepared (Fig. S2–5 and Fig. 2 ). The four NCDs had differing excitation-dependent and concentration-dependent PL emission responsiveness. F-NCDs showed the bright blue fluorescence, while H-NCDs, E-NCDs and D-NCDs emitted the weak green fluorescence at 365 nm excitation. The optimal PL emission peaks of F-NCDs were redshifted from 410 to 455 nm (range of 45 nm) with the concentration increased from 0.02 to 0.5 mg·mL−1, and the PL intensity reached its maximum at 0.05 mg·mL−1 (Fig. S2). In Fig. 2b-c, with increasing concentration, the optimal excitation wavelengths and emission peaks of H-NCDs and E-NCDs were redshifted, tuned from 425 to 525 nm (range of 100 nm) and 410 to 530 nm (range of 120 nm), respectively. Their PL intensity reached its maximum at 0.2 mg·mL−1. All the PL intensity of F-NCDs, H-NCDs and E-NCDs firstly enhanced and then weakened, indicating that the three NCDs can transform from AIE to ACQ at a specific concentration. For D-NCDs, the emission peak of D-NCDs shifted from 415 to 500 nm (range of 85 nm) (Fig. 2d) with the declined PL intensity (Fig. S5) due to ACQ. The magnitude of the concentration-dependent spectral shifts was gradually aggrandized from F-NCDs, D-NCDs, H-NCDs to E-NCDs due to diverse energy transitions of four NCDs by concentration modulation. The abundant amino groups in precursor would contribute to the narrow spectral shift by concentration regulation [22]. Compared to others, the narrow spectral shift of F-NCDs may be attributed to the excess amino groups of FA molecules. The PL mechanism behind the PL performances had been deciphered. The PL emission originated from synergitic intrinsic sp2 conjugated domain in carbon core (quantum confinement effect, QCE) [36] and extrinsic surface defect states (surface emission centers) [25]. The eigen states are the origin of optical absorption properties and the multiple surface defect states determine the excitation-dependent emission [37]. Herein, to be more specific about the PL origin of NCDs, more details on the surface chemical structure of NCDs were investigated by XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chemical structure on PL emission of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was conducted to further uncover the chemical compositions of NCDs (Fig. 3a-d and Fig. S7). Three pronounced peaks at around 284.8, 398.8, and 531.8 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3a and Fig. S7a-c). The high-resolution C1s surveys showed three distinguish peaks at around 284.6, 285.3, and 289.1 eV, corresponded to graphitic sp2 C (C=C/C-C), CO, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3b) [38]. Their N1s peaks could be deconvoluted into two peaks, amide N and graphitic N (-C-N), located at around 398.5 and 399.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c, Fig. S7g-i) [8]. Two dominant peaks existing at 531.6 and 532.2 eV in the high-resolution O1s spectra were assigned to CO and C-OH/C-O-C bonds (Fig. 3d, Fig. S7j-l), indicating the existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups. Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, the obtained NCDs were identified as a N-doped aromatic structure consisting of a carbon backbone connected with different contents of N- and O-containing heterocyclic structures and -NH2, -OH groups. The relative contents of chemical groups in the structure of NCDs were calculated and shown in Table S2 and Table 1 . From lignin to NCDs, the O/C ratio of NCDs decreased significantly after dehydration and carbonization (Table S2). The declining O content and incremental sp2 C content indicated that the growing graphitization degree and sp2 conjugated domain from F-NCDs, D-NCDs, H-NCDs to E-NCDs agrees well with the increased spectral tuning by increasing concentration. In contrast, ethanol possessing a low boiling point leads to elevated steam pressure, dehydration, carbonization and facilitated the graphitization of NCDs with restraining the integration of N. A tentative conclusion can be drawn that quantum confinement effect (QCE) played a significant role in the excitation-dependent and concentration-dependent behavior of PL.

The PLQYs results of the four NCDs were presented in Table S3. A high PLQY of 42.69 % was found in F-NCDs due to the increased graphitic N groups in structure. -C-N groups will increase the electron cloud density of π-conjugated system and reduce non-radiative emissions [26,36,39]. Fig. 4 shows the reaction progress of F-NCDs: the Schiff base reaction occurs between -NH2 of EDA and CO of FA, forming the intermediate, which further reacts with lignin fragments and FA to synthesize F-NCDs. In addition, the ample -NH2 in EDA and FA dominated the narrowed spectral tuning of F-NCDs with rising concentrations [24]. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the four NCDs were measured (Fig. 3e-h). The fitted biexponential decay curves of NCDs had two components, the fast τ1 and slow τ2 was correlated with the radiative recombination of eigenstates (π-π* transitions) and N- or O-related surface defects (n-π* transitions), respectively [12,39]. The F-NCDs possessed a large percentage of τ1 (B1, 65.73 %) than E-NCDs (B1, 50.92 %). Hence, the blue PL emission of F-NCDs mainly originated from the small conjugated domain in carbon core, while those of E-NCDs were dominated by the synergistic effect of two emission states. The spectral shifts of NCDs were controlled by the eigenstate and surface defect states with concentration increases, the large particle size and surface defect states responded to the large spectral shifts of NCDs.

The absorption spectra of four NCDs with different concentrations were investigated to further clarify the mechanisms behind PL performances. As shown in Fig. 5a-d, NCDs solutions exhibited a strong absorption peak between 250 and 300 nm that derived from π-π* transition attributed to the CC bonds in conjugated sp2 domain in sp3 matrix [40]. The peak at 300–400 nm arose from n-π* transition related to CO and conjugated C-N/C=N [36], while the weak peaks at around 400–500 nm were attributed from the transitions from surface defects to ground states. Their corresponding adsorption intensities are related to the conjugated domains in carbon core and surface defect states, respectively. The adsorption of F-NCDs significantly increased in the range of 300–350 nm at 0.1 mg·mL−1, and those of H-NCDs and E-NCDs showed obvious adsorption enhancement until 0.2 mg·mL−1. The absorption bond of D-NCDs was not sensitive to concentration changes until the concentration was 0.5 mg·mL−1. The optical band gaps (E g) of NCDs were calculated from the Tauc plots ((αhν)2 versus hν curves converted from UV adsorption spectrum), in which α is the absorption coefficient, h is Plank constant (6.63 × 10−34 J), and ν is the light frequency [41-43]. The E g of NCDs at different concentrations were obtained by measuring the intersection values on the x-axes and listed in Table S4. In the Tauc plots (Fig. 5e-h), the E g decreased gradually with increasing concentration. Their narrowed E g reduced in the following order: 3.42 eV (F-NCDs) > 2.27 eV (H-NCDs) > 2.51 (E-NCDs) > 2.49 eV (D-NCDs). Changes of E g and their PL emission peaks were showed in Fig. 5i. F-NCDs showed the narrow spectral shift but large narrowed E g. E g decreased slightly at below 0.05 mg·mL−1 because of the concentrated energy levels in F-NCDs, but the excessive energy densities and hydrophilic groups (-OH) on F-NCDs caused the increscent sp2 structure, small new energy gaps, and redshifted PL emission. The luminescence behaviors induced by concentration modulation were shown in Fig. 5j-m. The maximum energy difference (δE) between the π and π* orbital was 0.66 eV (F-NCDs), 0.73 eV (H-NCDs), 0.98 eV (E-NCDs) and 0.75 eV (D-NCDs). δEs were below 1.5 eV within the required value for triple carbenes [36]. The δE tended to decrease with increasing concentrations, which was related to the new energy gaps located at the tail of π-π* gap, resulting in the red-shift of PL emission [24,44,45]. Based on the above results, we proposed that controlling the nature of sp2 domain and the combined surface states contribute to tuning PL emission.

To further explore the fluorescence mechanism of NCDs, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and band gaps of the designed models were calculated. As shown in Fig. 6 , two models with different benzene rings numbers were designed to investigate the effect of QCE. The band gap decreased significantly from 4.03 to 1.62 eV with the benzene rings increasing from 7 to 14, declaring that the large sp2 conjugated domain of CDs caused the redshift of PL emission [39]. The different chemical groups would also affect the HOMO/LUMO energy level of CDs, and then change the band gaps between them [46]. Models containing 7 benzene rings with different chemical groups (-OH, -NH2, CO and -C-N) were also designed. The band gap slightly increased from 3.83 to 3.92 eV with the number of -OH groups changing from 1 to 3, narrower than that for a bare sp2 domain (4.03 eV). The addition of -C=O groups leaded to a sharp decrease of band gap (0.49 and 0.17 eV). When the -NH2 groups were added, the band gap slightly decreased to 3.97 eV, which was independent on the number of -NH2 groups. The band gap increased from 3.92 to 4.09 eV after grafting -C-N groups. The calculation results showed that the large sp2 conjugated domain and increased CO groups contribute to the reduction of band gaps and redshift of PL emission. Thus, it indicated that the synergy effect of small sp2 conjugated structure and grated -C-N groups endowed the blue PL emission of F-NCDs, and yet the large sp2 conjugated domain and decorated CO groups resulted in the green PL emission of H-NCDs, E-NCDs and D-NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of fluorescent film and anti-counterfeiting ink
ce:section.ce:para: The PL emission intensity at different pH of F-NCDs was shown in Fig. 7a. F-NCDs had great stability at the broad pH range of 4–10, but it decreased from pH =10 to pH = 12 and showed the lowest emission intensity at pH = 2. The pH responsiveness of F-NCDs derived from the surface functional groups such as -NH2, -OH, and -COOH. The dissociation of acidic groups or the protonation of basic groups [47] may change the non-radiative process that compete with the PL emission process, thereby affecting the fluorescence emission. The PL emission intensity of F-NCDs was also detected in KCl solution (0–1.0 M). As depicted in Fig. 7b, the PL emission intensities of F-NCDs remained constant at the wide concentrations of KCl (0.02–1 M), implying that F-NCDs could be applied in the high saline solution. The great pH and ionic stability of F-NCDs endow it excellent potential applications.

A facile one-step solvent regulation strategy-assisted solvothermal strategy was designed for preparing lignin-derived N-doped CDs (PLQY of 42.69 %) without additional acidolysis. The Schiff reaction between the -C=O of FA molecule and -NH2 of EDA contributes to the doping of nitrogen, mainly -C-N groups, inducing the bright blue fluorescence of F-NCDs. According to the fluorescent mechanism analysis (structure, optical properties and DFT calculations), the small sp2 conjugated structure of F-NCDs dominated the blue PL emission, and the synergistic effect of large sp2 conjugated domain and CO related surface states contributed to the green PL emission of H-NCDs, E-NCDs and D-NCDs. The obtained F-NCDs with excellent photostability were applied to prepare fluorescent film and printable ink for anti-counterfeiting. Our findings offered a feasible way for the green facile preparation of lignin-based CDs with prominent fluorescent features and a promising direction for the printable anti-counterfeiting application of lignin-based CDs. And the fluorescence tunability of biomass-based CDs and theoretical molecular simulation will be further investigated for their future advanced application.

Xiaohui Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Rendang Yang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources. Yang Wang: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Chen Cheng: Validation, Data curation. Danning Fu: Visualization, Software. Jie Sheng: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515111147) and State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (202309).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.126723.
